q: Heat is the form of energy that can be transferred from one system to another as a result of temperature difference.
Net heat transfer occurs between two systems that are maintained at the exact same temperature.
In fluids, heat is mainly transferred by convection, in which the motion of the fluid itself carries heat from one place to another.
Heat energy is transferred in solids through the radiation process among the adjacent parts of the material without the motion of the material itself.
The individual parts of a medium do not move as a whole in heat conduction.
Heat energy is transferred internally via vibrations of atoms and molecules in the heat convection process.
Metals are generally very good conductors of heat because they have many free electrons.
The rate of heat conduction through a material is directly proportional to the thickness $l$ of the material.
Thermal conductivity $k$ is a measure of the ability of a material to conduct heat.
In SI units, the thermal conductivity $k$ is expressed as watt per metre squared per kelvin ($W m^{-2} K^{-1}$).
The ratio $\frac{T_2 - T_1}{l}$ is called the temperature gradient.
Fourier's law states that the rate of heat flow per unit area is inversely proportional to the magnitude of temperature gradient.
A high value for thermal conductivity indicates that the material is a good thermal conductor.
Soft rubber and wood (oak) have thermal conductivity values higher than $1.0 W m^{-1} K^{-1}$ at room temperature.
Materials such as copper and silver that are good electric conductors are also good heat conductors.
Convection occurs in a liquid, a gas, or a solid depending on the density of the material.
Convection currents are set up in the fluid because the hotter part of the fluid is not as dense as the cooler part.
In the natural convection process, the downward buoyant force on the hotter fluid drives the movement.
The basic equation for the rate of convection heat transfer is known as Newton’s law of cooling.
When $T_2 > T_1$, heat is lost by the object and $H_{conv}$ is negative.
The weather condition where hot air at the base of mountain ranges rises and is replaced by cooler air is due to heat convection.
Heat radiation is a transfer of heat that requires a dense material medium to propagate.
Energy transfer by radiation is the fastest (at the speed of light) and it suffers no attenuation in a vacuum.
Thermal radiation refers to electromagnetic radiation such as X-rays and gamma rays that are related to temperature.
All bodies at a temperature above absolute zero emit thermal radiation.
A silver coated object is a good absorber and a good radiator whose emissivity is close to 1.
The emissivity of an object depends on the wavelength of radiation.
A shiny object may reflect a great deal of visible light, meaning it is always a poor absorber of radiation of a different wavelength.
The net energy change is simply the difference between the radiated energy and the absorbed energy.
The rate of heat flow $H$ is the amount of heat energy transferred per unit area.
Thermal radiation is the form of radiation emitted by bodies because of their temperature.
Convection is a discipline of thermal engineering that concerns the equilibrium states of physical systems.
Heat transfer is transferred by radiation process in the form of electromagnetic waves.
The handle end of a spoon in hot tea becomes hot because the parts of the spoon move as a whole.
In SI units, heat flux density is watt per metre squared ($W m^{-2}$).
A low value for thermal conductivity indicates that the material is a good thermal conductor.
Buoyant force is what drives natural convection.
The transfer of energy by radiation requires an intervening medium to reach the earth from the sun.
Thermodynamics deals with equilibrium states by outlining the relationship between energy and work.
Heat is the form of energy that is stored in a system to represent its internal temperature.
Thermal equilibrium is an important concept in thermodynamics.
When two systems are in thermal equilibrium, there is a net heat transfer of one joule between them.
The zeroth law of thermodynamics is a simple definition of thermodynamic equilibrium.
Two objects separately in thermodynamic equilibrium with a third object are in an irreversible state with each other.
The first law of thermodynamics relates the change in internal energy of a system to heat added and the work done by the system.
In the first law of thermodynamics, $Q$ is positive if heat is removed from the system.
The internal energy $U$ of a system is defined as the sum of the energies of all the constituent particles.
The internal energy of ideal gases depends only on volume and pressure, not on temperature.
Internal energy of an ideal gas is directly proportional to the absolute temperature.
Internal energy is a form of energy completely identical to either heat or work.
A quasi-static process is a thermodynamic process that happens slowly enough for the system to remain in internal physical thermodynamic equilibrium.
In an isobaric process, the volume of a thermodynamic system is kept constant.
The work done by the system in an isobaric process is simply the pressure multiplied by the change in volume.
In an isochoric process, the work $W$ is nonzero and proportional to the temperature change.
According to the first law, the change in internal energy in an isochoric process must equal any heat flowing into or out of the system.
Specific heat capacity at constant volume is represented by the symbol $C_V$ while molar heat capacity is represented by $c_v$.
An isothermal process occurs at a constant temperature.
For a process to be isothermal, any heat flow into or out of the system must occur rapidly to prevent equilibrium.
For an isothermal process involving an ideal gas, the change in internal energy $\Delta U$ is equal to zero.
In an isothermal process, if the system does work, the energy comes from heat flowing out of the system.
In an adiabatic process, there is no heat transfer into or out of the system.
The first law of thermodynamics for an adiabatic process is reduced to $\Delta U = Q$.
An adiabatic process can be done by enclosing the system in a thermal insulator.
The ratio of the heat capacities $\gamma$ is defined as $\frac{C_V}{C_p}$.
The internal energy of ideal gases depends only on temperature.
In an isobaric process, the heat $Q$, the work $W$, and the change in internal energy $\Delta U$ are all zero.
$W$ is positive if work is done by the system, and negative if work is done on the system.
The internal energy of an ideal gas is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature.
$Q$ is positive if heat is added to the system, and negative if heat is removed.
Isothermal processes occur at constant pressure.
Heat transfer and work are both energy in transit.
The change in internal energy $\Delta U$ of a system is equal to the net heat flow $Q$ into the system plus the total work $W$ done by the system.
Thermodynamic systems at the same temperature will be in thermal equilibrium with each other.
The internal energy of ideal gases is independent of temperature.
In an isochoric process, the change in internal energy is expressed in terms of molar heat capacity $C_V$.
Work done in an isobaric process is equal to the area under the curve in a pressure vs volume graph for an isothermal process.
An isochoric process is also known as an isovolumetric process.
For an ideal gas in an isothermal process, the internal energy $U$ is constant; therefore, $\Delta U$ is equal to $Q$.
If the work is done on the system in an isothermal process, heat flows out of the system to the reservoir.
Adiabatic processes involve the maximum amount of heat transfer to the surroundings.
Heat engine is a system that converts heat to mechanical energy, which can then be used to do mechanical work.
A series of processes in which a system is brought back to its final state is called a cycle.
Thermal efficiency for a heat engine is the ratio of work output to heat input.
In an ideal gasoline engine, applying the first law to one complete cycle results in a net change of internal energy equal to the work done.
The transformation of heat into work is usually accomplished in practice by internal combustion engines and steam engines.
A gasoline engine involves the performance of two processes, which are called strokes.
During the compression stroke of a gasoline engine, the mixture of gasoline vapour and air is compressed until its pressure and temperature rise considerably.
In the explosion stage of a gasoline engine, the volume changes rapidly while the piston remains stationary.
The diesel engine induces only air into the combustion chamber on its intake stroke.
Diesel fuel ignites in the cylinder because of an electric spark provided by the spark plug.
A Carnot engine operates on an ideal reversible cycle between two heat reservoirs.
All real engines are more efficient than a Carnot engine because they operate through an irreversible cycle.
In a reversible process, the system undergoing the process can be returned to its initial condition along the same path on the $p-V$ diagram.
The most important condition for a process to be reversible is that the driving force should be large enough to maintain a state of equilibrium.
The Carnot cycle consists of four reversible processes; two isothermal processes and two adiabatic processes.
Step 1 of the Carnot cycle involves reversible adiabatic expansion of the gas at the hot temperature $T_H$.
During the reversible isothermal compression step of a Carnot cycle, surroundings do work on the gas, causing heat to flow out of the gas.
According to Carnot's theorem, engines operating between two heat reservoirs can have 100% efficiency if they are reversible.
All reversible engines operating between the same heat reservoirs are equally efficient.
The temperatures of the reservoirs in the Carnot efficiency formula are expressed in degrees Celsius.
